text: In the life of every surgeon there will come a day in which an EEA stapler either fails or is not available and therefore in this video I'm covering how to do a fully hand-sewn end-to-end colonic anastomosis in a robotic fashion. And with the recent recall of Ethicon EEA staplers, although we have grown accustomed to and almost dependent upon, these things do have a failure rate and also it is imperative that we all have some knowledge of how to actually do these operations in an end-to-end fashion without the need for the actual stapler. So I want to run down that in this video here, but first some schematics. First off, if you can see by this schematic drawing that involves two open ends, starting sutures at the mid portion of the back wall of the anastomosis. This is followed by the graphic on the left, an internal running suture in a full thickness manner, running in to out, in to out, from the back wall, two suture lines, one heading, in this case, in the north direction, and in this case, heading in the south direction. There's a transition stitch that has to occur once you get to the corners. So at one point, it's a full thickness from lumen to lumen, and you encircle and go lumen to lumen again, but at some point, you have to transition, and this diagram is depicted in which, on the, let's call it the colonic side on the right here, that once that stitch comes through the right, you then backwall it out and begin what's called your canal stitch. And so this is where the canal stitch is being formed on the anterior wall. And the canal basically runs in a vertical fashion. in line with the bowel. Same thing on this northern suture and same thing on the southern suture. And this is a schematic drawing of what that anterior canal suture line looks like and when it's completed at the end.

The surgeon begins a hand-sewn colocolonic anastomosis by suturing the posterior wall at the midline, aligning the distal and proximal bowel ends. Using V-lock sutures, the surgeon runs a full-thickness suture from lumen to lumen, ensuring the suture is cinched down adequately without excessive tightness to avoid a tourniquet effect.

The surgeon continues suturing the anastomosis by advancing stitches intraluminally to extraluminally on the distal limb and extraluminally to intraluminally on the proximal limb, using a fenestrated bipolar instrument for guidance. The anterior canal is formed with running U-stitches, followed by the initiation of a second posterior suture line using a 3-0 V-lock and V-twenty needle, overlapping the initial suture line for reinforcement.

The surgeon transitions to the canal suture, taking full thickness bites from out to in and in to out, while carefully rolling the corners. The final canal stitch is completed, and the V-lock sutures are tied together. Running interrupted Lembert sutures are placed to reinforce the suture line, particularly at the intersecting point. An endoscopic view shows the anastomosis, with insufflation confirming no leaks.

And you can see the anastomosis is wide open. So that's it. Hope you enjoyed it, seeing demonstration of a fully hand-sewn intracorporeal anastomosis in an end-to-end fashion. I hope you don't have to be in this situation, but if you do, hopefully this video is of some help to get you through that case. Best of luck. Thanks. Take care.
